— A Letter from the Atelier

One designer, nine years, two hands.

I started Queen Mode in 2017 with a borrowed dressmaker's dummy and a single yard of antique muslin. The first gown took eleven weeks. Over a hundred and twenty pieces later, I still draft, cut, and finish every commission myself — for adults, and for children too.

The cloth is sourced from mills that have woven for the trade since the reign of Victoria. The embroideries are threaded by the women of Lunéville, in botanicals, ribbons, and small private symbols of the wearer's choosing.

— Vy Jameson Founder · Première · Sole Pair of Hands
— Letters from Wearers

What they said, in their own words.

Eleanor made my mother's wedding gown into something my daughter could wear. Three generations in the same cloth. I have never been so moved by a piece of clothing in my life.

— Beatrice, 62Restoration · MMXXVI

The fitting itself was an experience I won't forget. Two hours of tea and conversation. By the end I felt as though I had been measured for the gown of a person I was trying to become.

— Camille, 28Bespoke · Vienna debut, MMXXVI

My daughter's flower-girl dress matched my wedding gown exactly. She wore it for two days afterwards and refused to take it off. It will fit her cousin in three years.

— Mira, 34Bespoke adult · children's atelier · MMXXVI

I commissioned a christening gown for my son. Hand-finished hems, the smallest mother-of-pearl buttons I have ever seen, a piece I intend to keep for the rest of my life and pass down. The price was fair. The work was extraordinary.

— Helena, 31Children's atelier · MMXXV
— From the Cutting-Table

The Journal

Updated weekly
Monday · 04 May MMXXVI

Drafting the Eloise commission's bodice block.

Three hours on the toile. The growth allowance went into the side seams instead of the hem this time — a request from her mother, who wants the dress to last past her sister's wedding next May.

Friday · 01 May MMXXVI

The Lyon silk arrived, finally.

Six weeks late. The colour is, as always, slightly different than the swatch — softer, less yellow. I think the wearer will prefer it. I hope so. I have already cut the bodice.

Wednesday · 29 April MMXXVI

Hand-rolling thirty-eight inches of hem.

The Vesper gown's tulle hem, finished by hand at quarter-inch intervals. Six hours, one needle, no music. It is my favourite kind of working day.

— Correspondence

Letters from the Maison

Quarterly notes on cloth, ceremony, and the women we dress. No more than four pages a season.

— A Letter from the Workroom

Nine Years, Two Hands

Queen Mode is a one-woman atelier. Every gown that leaves this studio has been cut, draped, and finished by the same person who wrote this page.

— at the cutting-table, Bloomsbury, March MMXXVI
— From Eleanor

I started in 2017 with a borrowed dressmaker's dummy and a single yard of antique muslin.

The first gown took me eleven weeks. It was for a friend's wedding and it was, frankly, imperfect — the hem was a quarter-inch uneven on the left side, and you can still see it in the photographs if you know where to look. She wore it anyway. She kept it. And I started over the next morning.

Nine years later I've drafted around two hundred and forty pieces by hand. I've inherited fourteen patterns from a retiring couturière in Marylebone, photographed every page of an 1889 dressmaker's notebook found at a Sussex estate sale, and learned Lunéville embroidery from a woman in her eighties who still threads from a hooked needle held overhead.

I make Victorian-romantic gowns for grown women, and — this is the part most ateliers won't touch — I make them for children too. A communion dress. A flower-girl dress. A christening gown built to be passed down. The patternmaking is the same, the labour is the same, the seriousness is the same. The only difference is the hem.

Thank you for being here.

— Eleanor VanceFounder · Première · Sole Pair of Hands
9
years at the cutting-table
240+
finished gowns to date
14
inherited Victorian patterns
80–240
hours per piece, by one pair of hands
i.

The Adult Commission

For brides, mothers-of-the-bride, debutantes, and the woman who simply wants a gown the way her great-grandmother had a gown. Three fittings minimum, four to twelve weeks. Begins with a long conversation over tea.

ii.

The Children's Atelier

The unique work of this studio. Communion dresses, flower-girl dresses, christening gowns intended to be kept and re-worn through three generations. Hand-finished hems with seven inches of growth allowance, hidden buttons children can manage themselves.

— rarely offered elsewhere
iii.

The Restoration

Heirloom gowns brought back from attics and old wardrobes. I work with the cloth I'm given, replacing only what cannot be saved. Four to six months. Expect honest reports about what is and isn't possible.

— What a Commission Looks Like

The five visits.

  1. i

    The conversation

    An hour, in person if you're in London or Paris, by video otherwise. We talk about the occasion, the wearer, what they like, what they hate, what they've worn before. I don't take measurements yet.

  2. ii

    The cloth

    I send three to five swatches with notes on what each would do to the silhouette we discussed. You touch them, take a week, choose. A 30% deposit is taken at this stage.

  3. iii

    The toile

    A muslin mock-up of the gown, fitted to your body. We adjust the bodice, mark the hem, decide where the embroidery should fall. I go away and cut the real cloth.

  4. iv

    The fitting

    The gown in cloth, hand-basted, on you. We make whatever changes are needed. This is usually the longest visit. Tea is involved.

  5. v

    The unveiling

    You collect the finished piece, signed and numbered, in a hand-stitched garment bag of unbleached linen. You pay the balance. I write you a letter about the cloth and how to care for it.

The Workroom

London
14 Fitzroy Square
Bloomsbury · By appointment
Tuesday through Saturday

Paris Fittings

Once a Month
A borrowed room on rue Saint-Honoré
The first weekend of each month
By prior appointment only

Anywhere Else

By Post
Children's commissions and restorations
can be conducted entirely by post.
Write to the studio for the kit.
— The Maison

Couture

Hand-finished pieces of the present collection. Each one drafted, cut, sewn, and signed by the same person — over the course of weeks, sometimes months.

— Honest Pricing

What a piece actually costs to make.

Most ateliers don't tell you. I will. The numbers below are what each tier of work involves — the hours, the cloth, the embroidery — translated into a price. There are no membership fees, no gatekeeping, no reason to hide.

The Atelier Piece
€ 1,400 — 2,400
80 to 120 hours · existing pattern · two fittings · finished cloth from my mill list
The Bespoke Adult
€ 4,800 — 9,800
140 to 220 hours · pattern drafted to your body · three fittings · embroidery negotiated · cloth of your choosing
The Bespoke Child
€ 1,800 — 4,200
60 to 140 hours · pattern with seven inches of growth · two fittings · designed to be passed down
The Heirloom Commission
€ 9,800 — 18,000
240 hours and beyond · weddings, debutante balls, occasion gowns · hand embroidery throughout

— Nine Years of Work

The Archive

Not a catalogue. A diary. The pieces below are arranged by the year they were made, with notes on what was happening at the cutting-table and what changed about the work.

MMXXVI

The Riverbank — the present collection.

Forty-eight pieces conceived around the idea of "drowned florals," photographed in the conservatories at Kew. The first collection produced from the new Bloomsbury workroom. Fourteen pieces remain available; the rest are reserved or already gone.

Ophelia · 14 available
Seraphim · reserved
Vesper · 6 available
MMXXIV

The year I almost stopped.

Six commissions, two restorations, and a great deal of doubt. I made a wedding gown for a woman in Edinburgh that I think might be the best thing I have ever made; I made a christening gown for her sister's child four months later. By December I had decided to keep going.

The Edinburgh Gown · sold
Christening Gown · sold
MMXXII

The children's atelier opens.

A mother asked me whether I would make a flower-girl dress to match a wedding gown I was already drafting for her. I said yes, and then I said yes again to the next four mothers who asked. By the end of that year, a third of the workroom's output was for children. I have never marketed it; word travels.

The First Flower-Girl · sold
MMXX

Lockdown. Restorations only.

No fittings possible. I spent the year repairing heirloom gowns sent in by post. Fourteen of them. I learned more in that year than in the three before it. The work taught me how Victorian dressmakers actually solved problems — not from books, but from the seams.

MMXVII

The first commission.

A friend's wedding gown. Eleven weeks. A quarter-inch uneven hem on the left side. She wore it. She kept it. I started over the next morning. This is where Queen Mode begins.

The First Gown · archive only, not for sale
— The Wearers

Lookbook · Vol. III

Four pieces, four people who chose them. Photographed in the rooms where they were worn, on the days they were worn. No models. No retouching. The dresses do enough work on their own.

i.

Mira, 34, on the morning of her wedding.

She had eight months to plan it and asked for a gown her grandmother might recognise. The Ophelia silhouette in pressed muslin and silk chiffon, hand-embroidered cornflower along the hem — she chose cornflower because her grandmother grew them in Gloucestershire in the seventies.

The hem moved a quarter-inch through the morning ceremony. We re-pinned it before she walked down the aisle. She told me afterwards that the gown was the only thing she didn't worry about all day.

Ophelia Gown·€ 4,800 bespoke·118 hours
ii.

Eloise, 7, at her older sister's wedding.

This is the work that surprises people. A flower-girl dress in crushed-rose linen, drafted to match the adult wedding gown her sister was wearing — same bodice geometry, same hand-printed botanical, just smaller. Seven inches of growth allowance hidden in the hem so it can be kept and re-worn.

Her mother told me that Eloise refused to take it off for two days afterwards. She wore it to the breakfast table. She wore it to the garden. The dress will fit her younger cousin in three years.

The Children's Atelier·€ 2,200 bespoke·74 hours
iii.

Camille, 28, at her debutante ball in Vienna.

The Seraphim bodice in cloth-of-gold over hand-loomed jacquard, drafted from a private 1889 commission found in the archive of a retiring couturière in Marylebone. We took six weeks. She told me she wanted to look like she had stepped out of a photograph her great-grandmother might have posed for.

The gown weighs four pounds. She danced for three hours.

Seraphim Bodice·€ 7,200 bespoke·184 hours
iv.

Beatrice, 62, restoring her mother's gown for her daughter.

Three generations in one piece of cloth. The original was made in 1961 — a velvet evening gown with a tulle collarette, badly damaged by storage. I worked with what was salvageable: replacing the linings, rebuilding the boning, re-fastening the original mother-of-pearl buttons.

Her daughter wore it to her own engagement dinner this March. The bodice is, in places, sixty-five years old. Beatrice cried at the fitting. I will admit I did too.

Restoration Commission·€ 6,400·5 months
— A Note

If your story belongs in the next volume, write to the studio. The fifth volume is being photographed in spring MMXXVII; pieces are being commissioned now.

Queen Mode

est. mmxxv · london

Romantic couture in the Victorian tradition, drafted by hand at our Bloomsbury atelier and at our Paris workrooms on rue Saint-Honoré.

— Couture Piece

Ophelia Gown

€ 1,840

Hand-embroidered silk chiffon, drafted from the 1873 archive. Each piece numbered, signed by the première, and finished to the wearer's measurements over four to six weeks.

Queen Mode
OpheliaSeraphimVesperRosalind Children's atelierWedding gownsRestorationsVelvet

The Atelier

Track your commission's progress, view your toiles, and message the studio. Accounts are issued to active commissioners only.

— Step One of Five

Who is the gown for?

Adult and children's commissions follow different patterns and timelines.

— Step Two of Five

What is the occasion?

If your occasion isn't here, the last option works fine.

— Step Three of Five

And the budget?

An honest range so I can suggest the right tier of work. The numbers below correspond to the time the piece will actually take.

— Step Four of Five

Tell me about it.

A sentence is enough. Anything you want me to know — a date, a person, a memory, a fabric.

— Step Five of Five

How do I write back to you?

I write to every enquiry personally, usually within forty-eight hours. — E.

❦

Thank you.

Your enquiry has reached the studio. I will write back to you, by hand and from the cutting-table, within forty-eight hours. — Eleanor
